source: iConnections Global Alts 2024 — Industry titans panel: AI, growth/crossover investing
summary: AI scaling laws entrench hyperscale incumbents, making most standalone frontier model companies worthless
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI scaling laws — which require roughly 10x more compute and data to double algorithmic performance — structurally favor the largest hyperscale incumbents and will render nearly all standalone frontier model companies worthless over the medium term. The core reasoning is that only companies with massive compute infrastructure, proprietary data at scale, and large distribution networks for reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) can sustain frontier model development; without these, smaller model companies cannot compete. Baker identifies Google, Microsoft/OpenAI, and xAI/Twitter as the hyperscale winners, while also noting that high-quality open-source models will further squeeze standalone frontier model companies from below. The investment implication is to own the hyperscale incumbents with compute, data, and distribution moats, and to avoid or short standalone frontier model companies (most of which are private), as Baker states with high conviction that 'almost all are going to be zeros.' This view has been held for multiple years and was explicitly endorsed by fellow panelist Gracias during the discussion.
detail: Baker articulates that scaling laws (10x compute/data needed to double algorithm performance) mean the largest companies with the most compute and data win. Frontier model companies without massive distribution for RLHF will go to zero, squeezed between hyperscalers (Google, Microsoft/OpenAI, xAI/Twitter) and high-quality open source.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this as a multi-year held view, uses strong language ('almost all going to be zeros'), and Gracias explicitly agrees
quote_fragment: almost all going to be zeros if you're a frontier model company

ticker_audit.reasoning: Google is explicitly named as one of the hyperscale incumbents that wins under Baker's scaling law thesis due to its compute, data, and distribution advantages.
Microsoft/OpenAI is explicitly named as one of the hyperscale winners in Baker's thesis, benefiting from its compute infrastructure and OpenAI distribution partnership.

META is a hyperscale incumbent with massive compute, proprietary social graph data, and distribution at scale for RLHF — fitting Baker's criteria for winners even if not explicitly named in the quote fragment. META's open-source LLaMA also plays the role of squeezing frontier model companies from below, making it a direct beneficiary of this dynamic. [RE-AUDIT: Baker did not explicitly name Meta in connection with this specific thesis. The first-pass reasoning relied on inference ('fitting Baker's criteria') and Meta's open-source role, not a direct statement by Baker connecting Meta to this exact scaling law / hyperscale incumbent thesis. Per the strict test, inferred beneficiaries must be removed.]

Amazon/AWS is a clear hyperscale incumbent with massive compute (AWS), proprietary data, and distribution — fitting Baker's exact criteria for winners under scaling laws. Its omission from the original list appears to be an oversight given it is the third major hyperscale cloud provider alongside Google and Microsoft. [RE-AUDIT: Baker did not explicitly name Amazon in connection with this thesis. The first-pass reasoning explicitly acknowledged it 'was not original' and that its inclusion was an inference based on it being a hyperscale cloud provider. This is exactly the kind of addition the strict audit is designed to remove.]
